evidence_citations.excerpt: I’m barely sleeping and the project at work is overdue, so I keep stalling.
I try to keep the project moving but end up stalling, and I can't even get past two at night.
I'm too drained to focus on anything. I can't get past two at night.

persona_updates.new_value: I am not in control of my finances

evidence_citations.excerpt: Court date next month, alimony piling up.
I keep thinking about the numbers on the bill; every time I try to focus on the code, my mind drifts to the mortgage and the lawyer's fees.
I keep putting off the project because it's too much, and I can't sleep past 2 a.m. with all this on my brain.
I'm stuck, like a slow braise with the stove off, and I can't get past two at night.

transcript.text: Court date next month, alimony piling up. I’m barely sleeping and the project at work is overdue, so I keep stalling.

You're facing a lot right now, with the court date looming and alimony piling up, and it's taking a toll on your sleep and work. It sounds like the pressure is really getting to you, and it's hard to make progress on your project when you're feeling so overwhelmed. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind lately?

I keep thinking about the numbers on the bill; every time I try to focus on the code, my mind drifts to the mortgage and the lawyer's fees. I keep putting off the project because it's too much, and I can't sleep past 2 a.m. with all this on my brain.

You're really struggling to focus on your project because the financial worries are always there, lurking in the back of your mind, and it's like the numbers on the bill are haunting you. The pressure to get everything done, from the project to dealing with the court date and alimony, is taking a huge toll on your sleep and overall well-being. It's no wonder you're feeling overwhelmed and like you're not making progress, given how much is on your plate right now. Yeah, it's really tough to deal with all these stressors at once, and it sounds like you're feeling a bit lost on how to move forward with everything.

Honestly, every time I sit down to code, the numbers on the bill keep creeping back in, like a stubborn spice I can't remove. I try to keep the project moving but end up stalling, and I can't even get past two at night. It's all the same—like trying to finish a slow braise when the stove is off. I don't know if I can make it through this.

The image of that stubborn spice is really striking - it's like those numbers on the bill are always simmering in the back of your mind, making it hard to focus on your coding project. You're trying to make progress, but it's tough when financial worries keep creeping in. It sounds like you're feeling really stuck right now, and it's affecting not just your work but also your sleep. Yeah, it's like everything is at a standstill, and it's hard to see a way forward.

I keep staring at the code, but the bill numbers keep simmering in the back of my mind, like a stubborn spice I can't remove. I'm stuck, like a slow braise with the stove off, and I can't get past two at night. Maybe I should break the project into smaller steps, but I'm not sure if that will help.

You're sitting there staring at the code, but your mind keeps drifting back to those bill numbers, and it's like they're haunting you. It's no wonder you're feeling stuck and like you're not making progress, given how much is on your plate right now. Yeah, it's really tough to deal with all these stressors at once. Breaking the project into smaller steps might be a good idea, but it's also important to acknowledge that it's okay to feel overwhelmed and that you're not alone in this feeling.
I think breaking it into smaller steps might help, but I'm not sure. The bill numbers keep creeping back in, and I can't get past two at night. I need a plan that doesn't feel like another project.

You're feeling stuck and overwhelmed, and it's like those bill numbers are always lurking in the back of your mind. Breaking your project into smaller steps might help, and I'd like to propose a small, manageable task to get you started. Why not try committing to just 10 minutes of focused coding today, without worrying about the entire project? This might help you build some momentum and feel more in control.
I don't know if I can even spare ten minutes; the numbers keep drifting back and I feel like I'm just piling tasks on top of each other. Maybe I need a plan that doesn't feel like another project.

Let's try to identify one tiny task that you can complete in the next hour, like making a list of all the tasks you need to do. This might help you build some momentum and feel more in control. You could start by writing down all the tasks that are swirling in your head, and then pick just one to focus on. This way, you're not adding another project to your plate, but rather breaking down the existing tasks into smaller, manageable chunks.
I don't think making a list will help; the numbers keep creeping back in and I'm too drained to focus on anything. I can't get past two at night.

You're feeling really drained and overwhelmed, and it's like those bill numbers are always there, lurking in the back of your mind. It's no wonder you're struggling to focus on anything else, given how much is on your plate right now. Your sleep is suffering, and it's affecting your overall well-being. Yeah, it's tough to deal with all these stressors at once. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind when you're lying awake at night, unable to get past 2 a.m.?

It’s like my mind keeps simmering the bills even when I try to turn off the stove. I keep staring at the code but the numbers keep leaking in, and I can’t get past two. Maybe if I had a clear recipe I could slice this into manageable chunks.

The image of your mind simmering the bills even when you try to turn off the stove is really powerful - it's like those numbers are always lurking, waiting to creep back in. You're trying to focus on your coding project, but it's tough when financial worries keep leaking in. It sounds like you're feeling really overwhelmed and like you need a clear plan to manage everything. Maybe we can explore what a 'clear recipe' would look like for you, something that would help you slice your tasks into manageable chunks.

I can see the recipe analogy, but I'm not convinced there's a simple step that will make the bills go away. If I could break the project into 15‑minute blocks, maybe that would keep the numbers from creeping back in. Still, I'm not sure if that will help me get past two at night.

You're still feeling really uncertain about whether breaking your project into smaller chunks will help, and those bill numbers keep popping up in your mind. It's like they're always there, simmering in the background, and it's affecting your sleep and overall well-being. I can see why you'd want to find a way to manage them, like breaking your project into 15-minute blocks. That might help you stay focused and avoid getting overwhelmed. What do you think is the smallest step you could take right now to start feeling more in control of your project and your finances?

I could try a 15‑minute block, but I'm not sure it will stop the bills from creeping in. Maybe if I just jot down the numbers first, it might give me a moment to focus on the code. I still can't get past two at night, so I'm not sure if I can even get through a block.

You're still feeling uncertain about whether breaking your project into smaller chunks will help, and those bill numbers keep popping up in your mind. It's like they're always there, simmering in the background. Jotting down the numbers before starting a coding block might give you a moment to focus on the code, and that's a small step you could take right now to start feeling more in control. What do you think might happen if you do jot down the numbers, and then try to focus on your coding project for just 15 minutes?

common_candidates.label: Support
common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')

common_candidates.label: Closed Question

common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here to support this person as they struggle with feeling overwhelmed by their bills and coding project, and they need help finding a way to manage their tasks without feeling like they're adding another project to their plate. Given their resistance and uncertainty, I'll use the support and complex_reflection strategies to acknowledge their concerns and help them explore their own thoughts. The evidence shows that they're feeling drained and struggling to focus, with their mind constantly simmering on the bills, and they've mentioned trying to break their project into smaller chunks, like 15-minute blocks. My entry point will be to acknowledge their uncertainty and explore what might help them feel more in control, like jotting down the numbers before starting a coding block.

evidence_used.content: I could try a 15‑minute block, but I'm not sure it will stop the bills from creeping in
Maybe if I just jot down the numbers first, it might give me a moment to focus on the code
financial security

response.final_response: You're still feeling uncertain about whether breaking your project into smaller chunks will help, and those bill numbers keep popping up in your mind. It's like they're always there, simmering in the background. Jotting down the numbers before starting a coding block might give you a moment to focus on the code, and that's a small step you could take right now to start feeling more in control. What do you think might happen if you do jot down the numbers, and then try to focus on your coding project for just 15 minutes?
